_note: ORIGINAL content. Think C++ and the Google C++ Class outline informed coverage only; no prose, examples, or exercises were copied.
title: Loops
summary: Repeat work a known number of times or until a condition changes.
refresher: A `for` loop has a starting value, a condition, and an update. A `while` loop repeats while its condition remains true. Make sure the condition eventually becomes false.

blocks.body: A loop repeats a block of code. Use a `for` loop when you can describe the start, stopping rule, and update together. Use a `while` loop when repetition depends mainly on a condition.

Read a `for` loop in four steps: set the counter, test the condition, run the body, then update the counter. For a collection with three items, valid indexes are zero, one, and two, so the counter must stay below the size.

blocks.title: C++ writes out the pieces of Python's range
blocks.body: Python's `for i in range(3)` hides the counter update. C++ writes the same plan as `for (int i = 0; i < 3; i++)`: start at zero, stop before three, and add one.

blocks.caption: The counter begins at zero, prints once per pass, and stops before it reaches three.
Use a strict bound so the count is never used as an index.

blocks.body: For three elements, the valid indexes are zero, one, and two. When `i` becomes three, the corrected condition is false. The version using `<=` allows index three and steps past the vector. If you do not need an index, use `for (int value : values)`.

blocks.title: A loop needs a way to finish
blocks.body: A `while` loop can run forever when the value in its condition never changes. Point to the exact update that moves the loop toward its stopping condition.

blocks.why: The loop includes one through four because its condition includes the upper boundary.
Count stays equal to three, leaving the while condition true forever.
